Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after my extracurriculars or when I felt like it, but most of the time I just didn't feel like it. Sometimes I'd sit down with my laptop open but end up scrolling through other stuff instead. The 22 hours I supposedly studied were probably half actual studying and half just staring at the screen feeling bored.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go days without touching anything course-related, then panic a bit when something was due and try to cram. It wasn't really tied to deadlines in a productive way though—more like I'd notice a deadline had passed and feel guilty but still not do much about it. Regular study times just never happened for me.
Q3: Usually I logged in because I got an email notification or remembered that I probably should check if something was due. Sometimes my friends would mention an assignment and I'd panic-log in to see what I'd missed. It was never because I was excited to learn something—more like a vague sense of obligation that I couldn't shake.
Q4: Not really, no. I tried at the beginning of the semester to make it a habit, like checking it every morning with my coffee. But that lasted maybe a week before I just stopped caring. The LMS never felt like something I wanted to open, so it never became routine—just another thing I avoided.
Q5: The recorded lectures were actually okay because I could listen to them while doing other things, which suits how I learn better. I'd put them on while getting ready or during my commute. The discussion boards were sometimes useful when other students explained things in simpler terms than the professor did.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the readings and the extra practice materials. They felt so dry and I couldn't make myself care enough to get through them. I'd open a PDF, scroll through it without really reading, and close it. The optional stuff? Forget it—I never even clicked on most of it.
Q7: If something was quick and I could see an immediate point to it, I might actually do it. Like if a video was under ten minutes and directly related to an assignment, I'd watch it. But anything that felt long or vague in its purpose just made me zone out before I even started.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. Required ones at least had some guilt attached to not doing them, but optional ones? My brain just categorized them as 'not my problem.' Even the required ones often felt optional in practice since I ended up skipping a lot of those too.
Q9: Yeah, sometimes I'd download lecture slides or audio and then study from those on my phone when I was out. I also had a study group chat where people would share notes, so I'd read through those instead of going through the LMS materials myself. It felt less overwhelming than logging into the system.
Q10: There were times I'd listen to a lecture recording while walking to my extracurricular activities, just absorbing it without taking notes or anything. Or I'd talk through concepts with a friend over coffee. None of that would show up anywhere, but it was probably some of my more genuine learning moments.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download a lecture or some slides once and then never go back to the LMS for that topic. I'd just keep the files on my phone and flip through them occasionally. It was easier than dealing with the login and navigation every time I wanted to review something.
Q12: Pretty much from week three onward it got harder. I started feeling really anxious about how behind I was, which made me avoid the course even more. My extracurriculars were demanding too, and honestly the course material just bored me so much that facing it felt like a chore I kept putting off.
Q13: My anxiety definitely made things worse. I'd feel overwhelmed just thinking about how much I'd missed, so I'd avoid the LMS entirely. Being tired from other activities meant I had no energy left for coursework. And when I was confused about something, I'd just give up rather than try to figure it out.
Q14: All the time. I'd open videos and let them play while I was actually on my phone doing something else. Or I'd have readings open in a tab but be thinking about literally anything else. My eyes were on the material but my brain had checked out completely.
Q15: I postponed almost everything important. The bigger the assignment, the more I avoided it because it felt too overwhelming. I knew I should work on them, but my anxiety would spike and I'd convince myself I'd do it tomorrow. Tomorrow never really came until it was too late.
Q16: Deadlines would create these brief spikes of panic where I'd log in frantically, but often by then I'd already missed them or there wasn't enough time to do quality work. They didn't motivate me to work steadily—they just made me feel worse about myself when I inevitably missed them.
Q17: I didn't get much feedback since I didn't submit much work. When I did get feedback on the few things I turned in, it was usually too late to feel relevant. Honestly, feedback kind of made me more anxious because it reminded me of how much I was failing.
Q18: The structure felt too rigid for me. Everything was laid out in this linear way that assumed you were keeping up, but once I fell behind, it felt impossible to catch up. There wasn't flexibility for someone like me who was struggling to stay engaged from the start.
Q19: The long lecture videos made engagement harder because I'd lose focus halfway through. Having so many small assignments also felt overwhelming—like death by a thousand cuts. What might have helped is more interactive stuff or shorter, punchier content that didn't require so much sustained attention.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's trying but struggling, like clicking around looking for help or trying to understand what they're supposed to do. Or maybe they're anxious like me and keep checking the LMS without actually being able to make themselves do the work. Activity doesn't equal productivity.
Q21: That kind of makes sense to me. If someone is efficient or learns better from textbooks or outside resources, they might not need the LMS much. I wasn't exactly performing well, but I did learn some things from downloaded materials and conversations that never showed up in my login data.
Q22: A course log can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but it can't show if you were actually paying attention or understanding anything. It misses all the offline studying, the mental state you were in, and whether the time spent was actually productive or just anxious scrolling.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. For me, I rarely felt that this semester. I was mostly just trying to survive and avoid the anxiety of failing rather than genuinely connecting with the material.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much anxiety I was dealing with or how bored and disconnected I felt from the content. It wouldn't show the conversations with friends where I actually learned things, or the times I listened to lectures while doing other stuff. It just shows clicks, not what's happening in my head.
